narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 43.2% chance that you would not pass. This threshold triggered the rejection to help the university select applicants with the strongest projected outcomes. The model's decision hinged primarily on three key aspects of your profile: your undergraduate GPA, your third-year grade decile in school, and your LSAT score. Among these, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the most influential factor, actually working in your favor by suggesting a stronger potential for success than the model might have otherwise assumed. In contrast, your position in the second decile for third-year grades pushed the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure, as it signals a dip in performance relative to peers—typically, students who passed averaged around the sixth decile. Your LSAT score of 25.50 also nudged the outlook toward failure, falling well below the average of about 37 for those who succeeded on their first bar attempt. While your middle family income bracket aligns with a substantial portion of successful students and your choice to study full-time matches the norm for passers, these did not outweigh the concerns from your grades and test score. Compared to typical accepted students, who often enter with undergraduate GPAs around 3.24 and much higher LSATs, your profile appeared riskier in those core academic signals. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially given your solid undergraduate record that the model did credit positively. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 35 or above, as that could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening recent academic performance through targeted coursework or tutoring might also help elevate your standing in grade deciles. We encourage reapplying after addressing these areas, or exploring preparatory programs proven to lift bar passage rates for students in similar situations. Your path isn't closed—many have turned around predictions like this with focused effort.
